andrea-vance-stuff.name: Andrea Vance

connections.role: Formerly Fairfax NZ
United Future leader and Revenue Minister — Textgate 2013 (86 emails in 13 days; Dunne resigned; Vance's phone records and parliamentary swipe card data were improperly accessed)

facts.text: 'Textgate' (2013): exchanged 86 emails over 13 days with Revenue Minister Peter Dunne about GCSB leak inquiry; Dunne resigned; a subsequent inquiry improperly accessed Vance's phone records AND parliamentary swipe card movement data — a major NZ privacy scandal

andrea-vance-stuff.bio: Andrea Vance is a Northern Irish political journalist currently serving as a senior journalist at Stuff in Wellington. She grew up near Belfast, where her father edited three local newspapers. She worked for seven years as an investigative reporter at the British tabloid News of the World. When the phone hacking scandal broke, Vance was in the Glasgow newsroom and has denied any personal involvement, stating "no one had any idea it was going on." She subsequently worked as night news editor at The Scotsman before emigrating to New Zealand with her Kiwi husband. She became a prominent press gallery journalist, working for Fairfax/Stuff and later 1News. In 2013, she was at the centre of the Peter Dunne email scandal, in which minister Dunne resigned after refusing to release 86 email exchanges with Vance regarding a leaked GCSB report. Her phone records and parliamentary swipe-card data were improperly provided to the inquiry without her knowledge, prompting a major privacy breach complaint. She was named Political Journalist of the Year in 2025.
andrea-vance-stuff.methodology: Based on 10 articles scored by AI

anna-fifield-stuff.name: Anna Fifield

connections.role: Formerly Fairfax NZ

anna-fifield-stuff.bio: Anna Fifield (born 1976, Hastings) is a New Zealand journalist with an extensive international career. She studied English at Victoria University of Wellington and completed a postgraduate journalism diploma at the University of Canterbury. At 25, she moved to London and joined the Financial Times, where she spent 13 years as a foreign correspondent covering the Middle East, Korea, and US politics. She joined The Washington Post in 2014 as Tokyo bureau chief, later becoming Beijing bureau chief. She won the 2018 Shorenstein Journalism Award from Stanford and authored "The Great Successor" (2019), a book on Kim Jong Un translated into 24 languages. She was also a Nieman Fellow at Harvard. Returning to New Zealand in 2020, she edited The Dominion Post/Stuff's Wellington newsroom until December 2022. She returned to the Washington Post as Asia-Pacific editor but was laid off in February 2026. In March 2026, she joined The Spinoff to write the World Bulletin newsletter.
anna-fifield-stuff.methodology: Based on 0 articles scored by AI

audrey-young-nzherald.name: Audrey Young

connections.role: Political editor NZ Herald

audrey-young-nzherald.bio: Audrey Young is one of New Zealand's most experienced political journalists. She began her career as a sub-editor at the Auckland Sun, and after its closure in 1988 joined the New Zealand Herald as a sub-editor. She rose through the ranks to become the Herald's political editor, a role she held for 18 years (2003-2021), leading the paper's press gallery team in Wellington. In 2021, she transitioned to the role of Senior Political Correspondent, allowing her to focus on in-depth reporting. She was named Political Journalist of the Year at the Voyager Media Awards in 2018, 2020, and 2023. Her 30th anniversary at the Herald was acknowledged in Parliament. She has a particular interest in foreign affairs and Treaty of Waitangi issues, and served for six years as the union representative on the Press Council.

barry-soper-newstalkzb.bio: Barry Soper (born c. 1951, Gore) is the longest-serving member of New Zealand's Parliamentary Press Gallery, having joined in 1980. After briefly attending the Royal New Zealand Police College in 1969, he entered journalism at The Southland Times, progressing through the Mataura Ensign, Otago Daily Times, New Zealand Truth, and Waikato Times. He joined the press gallery working for APN before moving to Newstalk ZB, where he has served as political editor and senior political correspondent. He has covered eleven prime ministers, both Fijian coups (1987 and 2000), and Nelson Mandela's inauguration. He was named Individual Radio Journalist of the Year in 2001. In 1992, he was approached to stand as a Labour Party candidate in the Wellington Central by-election but declined. He married journalist and broadcaster Heather du Plessis-Allan in 2009. He was named unfavourably in Jacinda Ardern's 2025 memoir.

bernard-hickey-thespinoff.name: Bernard Hickey

connections.role: Economics columnist

bernard-hickey-thespinoff.bio: Bernard Hickey is a Wellington-based financial journalist, editor, and commentator with over two decades of experience spanning Reuters, the Financial Times Group, and Fairfax Media, working across London, Singapore, and Sydney. He co-founded and edited Interest.co.nz and Newsroom Pro. He is best known for an acknowledged ideological shift over his career: initially a conventional free-market economics commentator, he moved substantially leftward, becoming a vocal critic of New Zealand's housing policies, wealth inequality, and what he characterises as the failure of neoliberal settings to deliver for younger generations. His podcast with The Spinoff, "When the Facts Change" (named after the Keynes aphorism), reflects this evolution. He founded The Kaka, a Substack newsletter covering political economy, housing, climate, and poverty.
bernard-hickey-thespinoff.methodology: Based on 85 articles scored by AI

facts.text: Founded the Integrity Institute trust to fund investigative journalism, but it collapsed in 2025 after critics accused it of dirty politics - its trust deed targeted only right-leaning organisations and authorised media sting operations

bryce-edwards-stuff.bio: Bryce Edwards is a political commentator and academic based at Victoria University of Wellington, where he is a Research Associate in the Institute of Governance and Policy Studies and Director of the Democracy Project. He holds a PhD in political sociology (2004) and previously lectured in Political Studies at Otago University. Edwards has a well-documented history on the political left. He helped found the Canterbury University Progressive Left Club in 1993 and was a contact for the Canterbury University Marxist Society in 1995. He worked in the Alliance Party's electoral liaison unit in 2001 as an executive assistant. In 2007, he was identified as a member of the Workers Party and spoke at the Marxism 2007 conference. Despite this left-wing background, he has built a mainstream media career as a political analyst, writing regular columns for the NZ Herald, The Guardian, and other outlets. In 2025, his Integrity Institute collapsed amid accusations of funding journalists to produce pre-determined investigative stories targeting right-leaning organisations, with critics calling it the very 'dirty politics' it claimed to oppose.

connections.role: Political commentator and pollster

david-farrar-nzherald.bio: David Farrar (born 1967) is a New Zealand political blogger, pollster, and activist with deep ties to the National Party. He worked in Parliament for four National leaders, including in the Prime Minister's Office under Jim Bolger (1996-97) and Jenny Shipley (1997-99). He was national secretary of the Young Nationals. In 2003 he founded Kiwiblog, which became New Zealand's most widely read political blog. In 2004 he founded Curia Market Research, which serves as the National Party's internal pollster. In 2013 he co-founded the New Zealand Taxpayers' Union with Jordan Williams. He writes a regular column for the NZ Herald. The 2009 NZ Listener Power List ranked him the fourth most powerful person in New Zealand media.

Labour MP and Speaker of the House (married Dec 2014); served as NZ Ambassador to Ireland 2022-2024

facts.text: Married Labour MP and Speaker of the House Trevor Mallard in December 2014; Mallard served as NZ Ambassador to Ireland 2022-2024; Clifton had previously been in a relationship with National MP and Foreign Minister Murray McCully for approximately 20 years

jane-clifton-newsroom.bio: Jane Clifton is one of New Zealand's most experienced political commentators. She began journalism at age 16 in 1978 and spent roughly 20 years in Parliament's press gallery. She is best known for her political column in the NZ Listener, which she wrote for approximately 30 years. She married Labour MP (later Speaker of the House) Trevor Mallard in December 2014. She had previously been in a long relationship with National MP Murray McCully. Her writing is noted for its cross-partisan independence.

jane-patterson-rnz.bio: Jane Patterson is a senior journalist at Radio New Zealand who joined in May 2000. She spent most of her career in the Parliamentary Press Gallery, where she is a life member, including eight years as RNZ's political editor. She was appointed Director of Editorial Quality and Training at RNZ. Her awards include Best Coverage of a News Event for Election 2017 at the 2018 Voyager Awards.

jason-walls-nzherald.bio: Jason Walls is a New Zealand political and business journalist currently serving as business correspondent at TVNZ/1News. He holds degrees in economics and journalism from AUT. He began at NBR and Interest.co.nz before joining the NZ Herald's press gallery team in 2018, rising to political editor. He moved to Newstalk ZB in 2021, becoming political editor in 2023. Walls served as Chair of the Parliamentary Press Gallery. His wife, Jacinta Gulasekharam, co-founded Dignity NZ, a social enterprise combating period poverty.

jenna-lynch-stuff.bio: Jenna Lynch is a senior New Zealand political journalist. She began her career at the Waikato Times before joining TV3/MediaWorks in 2014, becoming Newshub's political editor in 2022 after Tova O'Brien's departure. She won Political Journalist of the Year at the 2022 Voyager Media Awards. Following Newshub's closure in July 2024, she moved to Stuff. Lynch's spouse is Andrew Ketels, who served as ACT Party chief of staff for five years and previously worked as a National Party ministerial adviser.

jessica-mutch-mckay-1news.bio: Jessica Mutch McKay is a former New Zealand television political journalist who served as TVNZ's 1News political editor. She joined Sky News in 2006 before moving to TVNZ in 2008. She hosted Q+A before becoming political editor in 2018, leading coverage of the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2023 general election. In early 2024, she departed TVNZ to become Head of Government Relations and Corporate Responsibility at ANZ Bank. Her husband, Iain McKay, is a Diplomatic Protection Service bodyguard who served on the personal protection detail of Prime Minister Ardern.

jo-moir.bio: Jo Moir is the political editor at Radio New Zealand, a role she took up in early 2024. She completed her postgraduate journalism diploma at Massey University in 2006. She worked at the Wairarapa Times-Age, Taranaki Daily News, and the Dominion Post before joining Stuff's press gallery team in 2015 and then RNZ in 2018. She subsequently served as political editor of Newsroom from 2021 before returning to RNZ. She has reported on five prime ministers and covered three general elections.

connections.role: Public broadcaster funded by NZ Government

Chief Correspondent at TVNZ/1News

john-campbell.bio: John Campbell (born 10 February 1964) is one of New Zealand's most recognised broadcast journalists. He began at Radio New Zealand as a business reporter in 1989. He moved to TV3 in 1991, co-anchoring 3 News (1998-2005). He fronted Campbell Live (2005-2015), whose cancellation prompted significant public debate. He returned to RNZ in 2016 to host Checkpoint, then joined TVNZ in 2018. In December 2025 he was announced as co-host of RNZ's Morning Report from April 2026. He is married to documentary editor Emma Patterson.

connections.role: Newstalk ZB broadcaster

kate-hawkesby-newstalkzb.bio: Kate Hawkesby (born 1 April 1973) is a New Zealand broadcaster, columnist and former television presenter. She holds a Political Studies degree and worked at London's Daily Express before returning to NZ. At TVNZ (1995-2007), she became the youngest person to present a One News at Six bulletin and co-anchored Breakfast with Mike Hosking in 2002. She hosted Newstalk ZB's Early Edition (5-6am weekdays) from 2017 until departing in November 2023. She is married to Mike Hosking. She is now an NZ Herald columnist and regular Friday panellist on The Mike Hosking Breakfast.

kerre-woodham-newstalkzb.bio: Kerre Woodham (born c. 1964) is a veteran New Zealand journalist, radio host, author and columnist. She studied journalism at Wellington Polytechnic and began her career at Radio New Zealand before moving into television as a reporter on Fair Go (1986-1990). She hosted Ready Steady Cook (1998-2005) and presented Heartland (1994-1997). An author of several books including Short Fat Chick to Marathon Runner, she is one of New Zealand's most recognisable broadcasters with over 35 years in media. She hosts Kerre Woodham Mornings (9am-12pm weekdays) on Newstalk ZB.

matthew-hooton-nzherald.bio: Matthew Hooton is a New Zealand PR consultant, lobbyist, and centre-right political commentator. He founded Exceltium in 2005, providing government relations and PR to major clients. He is known for his NZ Herald column and as a panellist on RNZ's political discussions. His dual role as commentator and lobbyist has drawn persistent conflict-of-interest criticism. In 2020, he was hired as a National Party consultant after helping engineer Todd Muller's leadership coup but resigned within months. He later settled a defamation claim brought by former National MP Steven Joyce.

max-rashbrooke-stuff.bio: Max Rashbrooke (born 1980, Wellington) is a New Zealand journalist, author, and researcher best known for his work on economic inequality. He studied at Victoria University of Wellington. From 2003 to 2010 he worked as a political journalist in London, including freelancing for The Guardian. He has authored four books on inequality, including Inequality: A New Zealand Crisis (2013). He has undertaken research contracts for organisations including the Green Party. In 2024, he co-founded the Institute for Democratic and Economic Analysis (IDEA).

mike-hosking-newstalkzb.name: Mike Hosking

connections.role: Host of Mike Hosking Breakfast on Newstalk ZB

mike-hosking-newstalkzb.bio: Mike Hosking (born 1965, Dunedin) is New Zealand's most prominent radio broadcaster. He left school at 16, starting as an advertising copywriter before getting his break on Wellington's Radio Windy. He hosted Radio New Zealand's Tonight Show and Morning Report, then joined Newstalk ZB in 2008 as weekday Breakfast host — a role he still holds. On television, he co-presented TVNZ's Breakfast (1997-2005) and Seven Sharp (2014-2017). He writes a regular opinion column for the NZ Herald. Hosking is an open supporter of the centre-right National Party and is frequently critical of Labour-led governments. His career has drawn multiple successful Broadcasting Standards Authority complaints. He is married to fellow Newstalk ZB broadcaster Kate Hawkesby. A landmark 2003 privacy case involving Hosking established "the Hosking principles" in NZ common law.
